metadata.summary: Three-tier VPC architecture on AWS: public ALB, private app tier (ECS/Fargate or EKS), isolated database subnets with Aurora Serverless v2 and security groups per tier.

content.title: AWS Three-Tier Architecture: VPC, ALB, App, Database
content.summary: The three-tier pattern places the Application Load Balancer in public subnets, application workloads (ECS Fargate or EKS) in private subnets, and Aurora Serverless v2 in isolated database subnets. Security groups enforce strict least-privilege ingress between tiers. Terraform modules orchestrate the full stack.

why.p: Separating tiers into distinct subnet classes closes lateral movement paths: internet traffic terminates at the ALB, application pods never receive inbound internet connections, and the database subnet has no route to the internet at all. Security groups that reference each other by ID (not CIDR) make the rules self-documenting and eliminate CIDR-drift bugs. Aurora Serverless v2 removes DBA provisioning toil while providing automatic scaling from 0.5 to 32+ ACUs per writer/reader instance.

list.item: Building new cloud-native web applications that need a standard HA production baseline.
Migrating on-premises three-tier apps to AWS with minimal architecture change.
Designing scalable microservices that still use a shared relational database.
Any workload requiring compliance isolation between network tiers (PCI, HIPAA).
Pure serverless workloads — use devops-aws-serverless-api instead; no EC2/ECS/EKS needed.
Single-AZ dev environments where cost matters more than HA — one NAT Gateway suffices.
Event-driven pipelines with no persistent HTTP layer — EventBridge + Lambda + SQS is simpler.

section.name: vpc-design
section.p: Public subnets hold only the ALB (and NAT Gateways). Private subnets hold application containers. Database subnets hold Aurora; they have no route to the internet. Use at least 2 AZs for every tier in production; 3 AZs is preferred.

rule.statement: Production VPCs MUST span at least 2 AZs for each subnet tier (public, private, database). Single-AZ deployments are forbidden for prod. NAT Gateways MUST be one per AZ in prod and one shared for dev/staging.
rule.rationale: AZ failure takes down all resources in that AZ simultaneously. A single NAT Gateway is an AZ-level SPOF that blocks all outbound traffic from private subnets if the AZ fails.

example.title: VPC with three tiers using terraform-aws-modules/vpc
example.code:
module "vpc" { source = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws" version = "~> 5.0"
name = "${var.project}-${var.environment}" cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"
azs = ["eu-central-1a", "eu-central-1b", "eu-central-1c"] public_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"] private_subnets = ["10.0.11.0/24", "10.0.12.0/24", "10.0.13.0/24"] database_subnets = ["10.0.21.0/24", "10.0.22.0/24", "10.0.23.0/24"]
enable_nat_gateway = true single_nat_gateway = var.environment != "prod" one_nat_gateway_per_az = var.environment == "prod"
enable_dns_hostnames = true enable_dns_support = true
# VPC Flow Logs enable_flow_log = true create_flow_log_cloudwatch_log_group = true create_flow_log_cloudwatch_iam_role = true flow_log_max_aggregation_interval = 60
# Tags for Kubernetes public_subnet_tags = { "kubernetes.io/role/elb" = 1 } private_subnet_tags = { "kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb" = 1 } }

section.name: alb
section.p: The ALB lives in public subnets. HTTP on port 80 redirects to HTTPS. The HTTPS listener uses TLS 1.3 policy. Access logs go to S3. Security group egress is limited to the VPC CIDR.

rule.statement: ALB listeners MUST redirect HTTP 80 to HTTPS 443 with status 301. SSL policy MUST be ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06 or newer. ALB access logs MUST be enabled.
rule.rationale: HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect at the load balancer prevents plaintext traffic from reaching the app. TLS 1.3-only policy drops broken legacy ciphers. Access logs are required for forensics and compliance.

example.title: ALB with TLS 1.3, HTTP redirect, and access logs

section.name: aurora
section.p: Aurora Serverless v2 uses db.serverless instance class. The writer/reader pair lives in the database subnet group. KMS encryption, Performance Insights, and IAM authentication are always enabled. Backup retention is 30 days in prod, 7 in non-prod.

rule.statement: Aurora clusters MUST enable storage_encrypted with a customer-managed KMS key, enable performance_insights, set deletion_protection=true in prod, and use iam_database_authentication_enabled=true. Backup retention MUST be ≥30 days in prod.
rule.rationale: Customer-managed KMS enables key revocation for compliance. IAM authentication eliminates static DB passwords. Deletion protection prevents accidental data loss from a Terraform mistake.

example.title: Aurora Serverless v2 PostgreSQL with KMS and IAM auth

section.name: security-groups
section.p: Three security groups — ALB, app, database — reference each other by ID. The database security group has no egress rules. App security group allows inbound only from the ALB security group on port 8000. Database security group allows inbound only from the app security group on port 5432.

example.title: Security groups referencing each other by ID

checklist.check: Public subnets hold only ALB and NAT Gateways; no app workloads.
Private subnets for app tier; no internet route.
Database subnets: no route to internet, no NAT gateway attachment.
At least 2 AZs per tier in production (3 preferred).
One NAT Gateway per AZ in prod; single for dev/staging.
ALB: HTTP redirects to HTTPS, TLS 1.3 policy applied.
ALB access logs written to S3.
Aurora KMS encrypted, Performance Insights on, IAM auth enabled.
Security groups reference each other by ID, not CIDR.
Database security group has no egress rules.
